title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Create isolated Python environment and install all required packages for causal discovery experiments. All experiments run on CPU only.

steps.step1: Create virtual environment: python3 -m venv venv && source venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install core dependencies: pip install numpy scipy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn pandas
steps.step3: Install causal discovery packages: pip install causal-learn gCastle networkx
steps.step4: Install optimization and metrics: pip install cvxpy statsmodels
steps.step5: Verify installations by running: python -c 'import numpy; import causallearn; import networkx; print("OK")'

title: Generate Synthetic Benchmark Datasets
description: Generate synthetic DAGs with varying properties for systematic evaluation. Graph sizes chosen for CPU-only feasibility.

steps.step1: Define graph parameters: n_nodes ∈ {10, 20, 30}, edge_density ∈ {1.0, 2.0} (expected edges per node), graph_types = ['ER', 'SF']
steps.step2: Define sample sizes: N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500, 1000} - focus on small sample regime
steps.step3: Define data generating mechanisms: (a) Linear Gaussian with weights ∈ [-2, -0.5] ∪ [0.5, 2], noise std=1, (b) Linear non-Gaussian using Gumbel distribution, (c) Nonlinear using sine and quadratic functions, (d) ANM with Gaussian noise
steps.step4: Generate 50 random graphs per configuration (n_nodes × edge_density × graph_type) using random seeds 1-10
steps.step5: For each graph, generate datasets with sample sizes N using random seeds 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (total 5 runs per configuration)
steps.step6: Save ground truth adjacency matrices to data/processed/ground_truth/{graph_id}.npy
steps.step8: Compute and save dataset statistics: mean, std, correlation matrices for verification

title: Prepare Real-World Datasets
description: Download and preprocess real-world benchmark datasets with known ground truth.

steps.step1: Download Sachs protein signaling dataset: 853 samples, 11 proteins, ground truth from literature
steps.step2: Preprocess Sachs: log-transform, standardize, handle missing values (impute with mean)
steps.step3: Load/construct Insurance benchmark (27 variables, known structure from literature)
steps.step4: Verify real-world data shapes: Sachs (853, 11), Insurance (N to be generated, 27)
steps.step5: Save processed datasets to data/processed/real_world/{dataset_name}.npz with keys 'data', 'true_dag'

title: PC Algorithm Baseline
description: Run PC constraint-based algorithm as simple baseline. Uses conditional independence testing.

steps.step1: Import PC from causallearn.search.ConstraintBased.PC
steps.step2: Set hyperparameters: alpha=0.05 (significance level), indep_test='fisherz' for continuous data, stable=True for order-independent output
steps.step3: For each synthetic dataset (n_nodes ∈ {10, 20, 30}, N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500, 1000}, 5 seeds): run PC algorithm
steps.step4: Record runtime for each run
steps.step5: Compute metrics against ground truth: SHD (Structural Hamming Distance), TPR (True Positive Rate), FDR (False Discovery Rate)
steps.step6: Save predictions to results/synthetic/pc/{config_id}.npz with keys 'prediction', 'runtime', 'metrics'
steps.step7: Run on real-world datasets (Sachs, Insurance) with same hyperparameters

title: NOTEARS Baseline
description: Run NOTEARS differentiable baseline for continuous optimization comparison.

steps.step1: Implement or import NOTEARS linear: minimize least squares loss with acyclicity constraint h(W) = tr(exp(W∘W)) - d = 0
steps.step2: Set hyperparameters: lambda1=0.1 (sparsity), lambda2=0.0, max_iter=100, h_tol=1e-8, rho_max=1e+16, w_threshold=0.3
steps.step3: Use L-BFGS-B optimizer for CPU efficiency
steps.step4: For each synthetic dataset: run NOTEARS with 5 random seeds (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) for initialization
steps.step5: Record runtime per run
steps.step6: Compute metrics: SHD, TPR, FDR, number of iterations to convergence
steps.step8: Run on real-world datasets, save to results/real_world/notears_{dataset}.json

title: GOLEM Baseline
description: Run GOLEM likelihood-based method as strong recent differentiable baseline.

steps.step1: Implement GOLEM-EV (equal variance) variant: maximize likelihood with sparsity and DAG penalties
steps.step2: Set hyperparameters: lambda1=0.02 (L1 penalty), lambda2=5.0 (DAG penalty), equal_variances=True, num_iter=10000, learning_rate=0.001
steps.step3: Use Adam optimizer with early stopping if loss plateaus
steps.step4: For each synthetic dataset: run GOLEM with 5 random seeds for weight initialization
steps.step5: Record runtime and final loss value
steps.step6: Compute metrics: SHD, TPR, FDR, final acyclicity constraint value
steps.step8: Run on real-world datasets, save to results/real_world/golem_{dataset}.json

title: Implement SPICED Core Components
description: Implement the three-phase SPICED algorithm: IT skeleton discovery, structural constraint extraction, and constrained optimization.

steps.step1: Implement Phase 1 (IT Skeleton Discovery): k-NN entropy estimation using Kraskov-Stogbauer-Grassberger method with k=5, compute directed information I(X→Y) using conditional mutual information, adaptive thresholding with permutation testing (100 permutations)
steps.step2: Implement Phase 2 (Structural Constraints): compute edge-connectivity using NetworkX, approximate feedback vertex set using greedy algorithm, extract modular decomposition if needed
steps.step3: Implement Phase 3 (Constrained Optimization): extend NOTEARS with structural constraint term c(W), formulate: min_W L(W) + λ₁h(W) + λ₂r(W) + λ₃c(W)
steps.step4: Implement warm-start: initialize W using IT scores from Phase 1
steps.step5: Set SPICED hyperparameters: k_neighbors=5, alpha=0.05, lambda1=0.1, lambda2=0.0, lambda3=0.01, max_iter=100, w_threshold=0.3
steps.step6: Create modular codebase: src/phase1_skeleton.py, src/phase2_constraints.py, src/phase3_optimization.py, src/spiced_main.py
steps.step7: Unit test each component on small 5-node graph with known structure

title: Run SPICED on Synthetic Benchmarks
description: Evaluate SPICED on full synthetic benchmark suite across all configurations.

steps.step1: For each synthetic dataset configuration (n_nodes ∈ {10, 20, 30}, graph_type ∈ ['ER', 'SF'], edge_density ∈ [1.0, 2.0], mechanism ∈ ['linear_gaussian', 'linear_nongaussian', 'nonlinear', 'anm'], N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500, 1000}, seed ∈ {1,2,3,4,5}): run SPICED
steps.step2: Record per-phase runtime: t_skeleton, t_constraints, t_optimization, t_total
steps.step3: Record intermediate outputs: skeleton from Phase 1, structural parameters from Phase 2, optimization trajectory
steps.step4: Compute metrics: SHD, TPR, FDR, SID (for graphs < 20 nodes), F1 score
steps.step5: Save detailed results to results/synthetic/spiced/{config_id}.npz with all metrics and timing
steps.step6: Save summary statistics to results/synthetic/spiced_summary.csv with columns: method, n_nodes, graph_type, mechanism, N, seed, SHD, TPR, FDR, runtime

title: Run SPICED on Real-World Datasets
description: Evaluate SPICED on Sachs and Insurance benchmarks.

steps.step1: Run SPICED on Sachs dataset with 5 random seeds, record runtime and metrics
steps.step2: Run SPICED on Insurance dataset with 5 random seeds
steps.step3: Compare predicted edges against known ground truth for Sachs
steps.step4: Compute SHD, TPR, FDR for each run
steps.step5: Save results to results/real_world/spiced_sachs.json and spiced_insurance.json

title: Ablation: Phase 1 MI Estimation Method
description: Compare k-NN entropy estimation (SPICED) vs kernel-based MI estimation to validate Phase 1 design.

steps.step1: Implement kernel-based MI estimator using scipy.stats.gaussian_kde as alternative
steps.step2: Run SPICED with k-NN estimation on subset: n_nodes ∈ {10, 20}, N ∈ {50, 100, 200}, 5 seeds
steps.step3: Run SPICED-variant with kernel-based estimation on same subset
steps.step4: Compare: SHD, TPR, FDR, runtime between k-NN and kernel methods
steps.step5: Statistical test: paired t-test on SHD differences across configurations

title: Ablation: Structural Constraints Effect
description: Measure impact of structural constraint term c(W) on optimization quality.

steps.step1: Run SPICED with full structural constraints (lambda3=0.01)
steps.step2: Run SPICED without structural constraints (lambda3=0.0), keeping Phase 1 and 2 but not using constraints in optimization
steps.step3: Run on subset: n_nodes ∈ {10, 20, 30}, graph_type=['ER'], N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500}, 5 seeds
steps.step4: Compare: SHD, convergence iterations, final acyclicity constraint value h(W)
steps.step5: Measure: number of times optimizer gets stuck in local minima (defined as h(W) > 1e-4 after max_iter)

title: Ablation: Information-Theoretic Initialization
description: Compare IT-based warm start vs random initialization.

steps.step1: Run SPICED with IT initialization (Phase 1 scores as initial weights)
steps.step2: Run SPICED with random initialization (standard Gaussian, 5 different seeds)
steps.step3: Test on subset: n_nodes ∈ {10, 20}, N ∈ {50, 100, 200}, 5 seeds
steps.step4: Compare: SHD, convergence speed (iterations to h(W) < 1e-6), final SHD
steps.step5: Statistical test: paired t-test on SHD differences

title: Sensitivity Analysis: Hyperparameters
description: Analyze sensitivity of SPICED to key hyperparameters.

steps.step1: Vary k_neighbors ∈ {3, 5, 7, 10} for entropy estimation, test on n=20, N=100
steps.step2: Vary lambda1 (sparsity) ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2}, test on n=20, N=100
steps.step3: Vary lambda3 (structural constraint) ∈ {0.0, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1}, test on n=20, N=100
steps.step4: Vary w_threshold ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4} for edge selection
steps.step5: For each parameter, run on 10 different graphs with 3 seeds each
steps.step6: Plot SHD vs parameter value to identify sensitivity

title: Scalability Analysis
description: Measure runtime scaling with graph size and sample size.

steps.step1: Run SPICED on n_nodes ∈ {5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50} with fixed N=500
steps.step2: Run SPICED on N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000} with fixed n=20
steps.step3: Record breakdown: Phase 1 time (should scale as O(n²N log N)), Phase 2 time, Phase 3 time
steps.step4: Fit scaling curves: t = a·n^b, t = c·N^d
steps.step5: Verify SPICED runs in < 5 minutes for n=50 on 2 CPU cores (success criterion)

title: Statistical Comparison of Methods
description: Perform rigorous statistical tests to validate success criteria.

steps.step1: Load all synthetic results for PC, NOTEARS, GOLEM, SPICED
steps.step2: Primary criterion 1: For N ≤ 200, compare SPICED vs NOTEARS SHD on each of 4 mechanisms. Use paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test (non-parametric). Success if p < 0.05 and SPICED has lower median SHD for ≥3 mechanisms.
steps.step3: Primary criterion 2: Check SPICED runtime for n=50 graphs. Success if median runtime < 300 seconds (5 minutes).
steps.step4: Primary criterion 3: Check SPICED SHD on Sachs dataset. Success if median SHD < 10.
steps.step5: Compute effect sizes: Cohen's d for SHD differences between methods
steps.step6: Create summary table: method × metric (mean±std SHD, TPR, FDR, runtime)
steps.step7: Save statistical test results to results/statistical_tests.json

title: Sample Efficiency Analysis
description: Detailed analysis of sample efficiency claim: SPICED superior for N < 500.

steps.step1: Group results by sample size N ∈ {50, 100, 200, 500, 1000}
steps.step2: For each N, compute mean and std of SHD across all graph configurations
steps.step3: Plot SHD vs N for all methods (log scale on x-axis)
steps.step4: Identify crossover point where NOTEARS/GOLEM catch up to SPICED
steps.step5: Test hypothesis: SPICED advantage decreases with increasing N using correlation test

title: Robustness Analysis
description: Test robustness across different data generating mechanisms and noise levels.

steps.step1: Analyze performance variance across 5 random seeds for each configuration
steps.step2: Compare performance across mechanisms: linear_gaussian, linear_nongaussian, nonlinear, anm
steps.step3: Test on graphs with latent confounders: generate data with 10% hidden variables, compare SPICED vs FCI
steps.step4: Analyze failure cases: identify configurations where SPICED performs poorly (SHD > 2× median)
steps.step5: Save robustness metrics to results/robustness_analysis.json

title: Generate Main Results Figures
description: Create publication-quality figures for paper.

steps.step1: Figure 1 (Sample Efficiency): Line plot of SHD vs N (50-1000) for SPICED, NOTEARS, GOLEM, PC. Separate panels for ER and SF graphs. Include error bars (std across seeds).
steps.step2: Figure 2 (Scalability): Log-log plot of runtime vs n_nodes for all methods. Include fitted scaling curves.
steps.step3: Figure 3 (Ablation): Bar chart comparing SHD for full SPICED, no IT init, no structural constraints, kernel MI. Grouped by sample size.
steps.step4: Figure 4 (Real-World): Comparison of predicted vs true Sachs network as adjacency matrices for SPICED and baselines.
steps.step5: Save all figures to results/figures/ as PDF (vector) and PNG (raster)
steps.step6: Use consistent color scheme: SPICED=blue, NOTEARS=red, GOLEM=green, PC=orange, FCI=purple

title: Generate Summary Tables
description: Create LaTeX-ready tables for paper.

steps.step1: Table 1 (Main Results): Methods × [SHD, TPR, FDR, Runtime] for each (n_nodes, N) combination. Report mean ± std across seeds and graphs.
steps.step2: Table 2 (Sample Efficiency): Focus on N ≤ 200. Show relative improvement of SPICED over baselines.
steps.step3: Table 3 (Ablation Study): Component × [SHD, TPR, FDR] showing impact of each design choice.
steps.step4: Table 4 (Real-World): Results on Sachs and Insurance datasets.
steps.step5: Table 5 (Hyperparameter Sensitivity): Range and optimal values.
steps.step6: Save tables as LaTeX code to results/figures/tables.tex and CSV to results/tables/

title: Generate Supplementary Figures
description: Create additional figures for appendix.

steps.step1: Figure S1: Convergence curves for Phase 3 optimization (loss vs iteration) for different initializations.
steps.step2: Figure S2: Heatmap of SHD across (n_nodes, N) grid for SPICED.
steps.figure_S3: Figure S3: Distribution of SHD differences (SPICED - NOTEARS) across configurations.
steps.figure_S4: Figure S4: Runtime breakdown by phase for SPICED across graph sizes.
steps.figure_S5: Figure S5: Effect of lambda3 (structural constraint weight) on final DAG quality.

title: Final Success Criteria Verification
description: Verify all success criteria are met and document any failures.

steps.step1: Check Primary Criterion 1: SPICED SHD < NOTEARS SHD for N ≤ 200 on ≥3 mechanisms. Document p-values and effect sizes.
steps.step2: Check Primary Criterion 2: SPICED runtime < 5 min for n=50. Document median runtime across 5 runs.
steps.step3: Check Primary Criterion 3: SPICED SHD < 10 on Sachs. Document median SHD and comparison to baselines.
steps.step4: Check Secondary Criteria: structural constraints improve convergence, IT init outperforms random, method works across data types.
steps.step5: Document any failure modes encountered: e.g., N < 50 unreliable, dense graphs problematic.
steps.step6: Create summary report: results/success_criteria_report.md with PASS/FAIL for each criterion
